text: AIN AMUR, a spring, overshadowed by some date-trees, on the route from the Great Oasis to the Wah el Dákhileh in Libya. Cailliaud says that the valley of Dakhileh is divided from that of Ain Amur by an elevated platform of calcareous rock about 30 m. in breadth. There are the remains of a temple near it in a small plain confined on three sides by mountains, and elevated 1,215 feet above the level of the valley.
AIN EL ARUS, a warm spring near the mouth of the Wadi Arabah, at the southern end of the Dead sea. It has a temperature of 95 deg .- Bertou.
AIN AZEL, a spring in the district of Selim in Algiers, at the head of the Wadi al Barikah, which flows SW into the sandy salt marsh of Al Shot.
AIN BAIATH, a v. of Algiers, on the SW borders of the salt lake called Al Shot, 130 m. SSE of Algiers.

AIN EL BALATAH, a considerable stream north of Bahr el Huleh, whose waters "run sluggishly to the eastward." AIN EL BARIDAH, "some half-dozen springs, one or two of which yield a copious supply of water," at the mouth of a wadi running down from the plain of Hattin to the W, 2 m. from Tiberias and 2 m. S of Majdil. [Wilson.] Irby and Mangles describe them as "Roman baths of mineral water, but only of a lukewarm temperature;" of a circular form, from 15 to 20 ft. in diam., and enclosed with a wall about 12 ft. high within and 6 without. The name signifies 'Cold fountain; but Dr. Wilson observes that this must be in contradistinction to the thermal springs of Tiberias.

AIN EL BELEDI, a station in the northern Libyan oasis, 5 m. from El Kasr, on the route to Siwah. AIN BROOT, AIN YEBRUD, or ANABROOT, a v. in the pashalik of Damascus, 12 hours N of Jerusalem, finely situated upon an eminence overlooking a rich scene of "olive and fig-gardens, vineyards and corn-fields overspreading the valleys and terraced on the hills,"-Lindsay. Alt. 2,354 ft.- Wildenbruch.
AIN EL BUKHARA, a fountain at the head of the valley of Mitree, in the Libanus district of Syria.
AIN EL COOM, a v. of Syria, 55 m. S of Rakka, at the head of the Soaid river, a branch of the Khabur.
AIN EDH DHAHAB, a copious spring near the sources of the Jordan, SW by W of the Tell el Haih, a basaltic ridge about 200 ft. high, and flowing towards its base. - Wilson.
AIN ED DIRWAH, a fountain to the NW of Beit-Ainún, on the road from Hebron to Bethlehem in Palestine.
AIN DISEH, a v. in the Great oasis, on the route between Darfur and Upper Egypt, in N lat. 26 deg. 40 min.
AIN EFDJUR, a v. in the pashalik of Acre, at the foot of the Antilibanus, 5 m. W of Ratheia.

AIN FIJJI, a pleasant v. at the extremity of a valley opening into the vale of the Barrada, 7 hours NW of Damascus. The Fijji here issues, in a large clear stream, of about 30 ft. wide, from under a limestone rock, and after coursing down a stony and rugged channel for about 100 yards, falls into the Barrada. Dr. Richardson describes it as one of the coolest and shortest rivers in the world.
AIN EL FORAT, a group of thermal springs, 5 m. W of Erzrum, from which one of the head-springs of the Euphrates takes its rise. They are also called ILIJAH or 'the Warm baths.'—Ouseley.
AIN GHADHYAN, a halting-place in the Wadi Arabah, an hour's journey to the S of a range of hills which crosses the wadi and separates the two basins of the Dead sea and the gulf of Akabah.—Bertou.
AIN EL GHAMAR, a spring of the temperature of 59 deg., on the W side of the Wadi Arabah, in N lat. 30 deg. 30 min. Bertou says both the smell and taste of the water of this spring were detestable.
AIN GHARANDEL, a spring in the Wadi Arabah, 35 m. S of the above, near the probable water-shed between the Dead sea and the gulf of Akabah.
AIN EL HAFIREH, a small spring of tolerable water in the Wadi Arabah, at the spot where the road from Hebron to Petra crosses the wadi, 8 m. N of Ain el Ghamar.—Bertou.

AIN EL HAUD, or HAUZ, a small stream of Turkey in Asia, in the pashalik of Jezira, which, descending from the southern flanks of the Gudji Dagh, flows in a SE direction through the district of Meiafarikin, and joins the Karasu on the r. bank. It is probably the ancient Nymphius.
AIN OU HEIDE, an intermitting fountain to the SE of the Jebel Auress in Algiers, which is said to discharge "a very plentiful flux of water into the Bagai."-Shaw.
AIN EL HOWASH, a small stream watering that part of the valley of the Orontes which is called El Ghab. Near Howash, the principal v. of the Ghab, it forms a small lake.
AIN JOWARI, a v. on the S coast of Arabia, W of Ras el Asidah.
AIN KARIM, a v. and convent of the Eastern Latin church, 5 m. W of Jerusalem.
AIN KITRAHN, a naphtha fountain, on the E side of Wadi Jinen, in the district of Zououiah in Algiers.
AIN MUSA, a v. and group of wells on the coast of Yemen, 23 m. N from Mokha.-Also a fountain at the head of Wadi Musa, to the E of the ruins of Petra. AIN
AIN-EL-RAZEE, a spring on the route from Aleppo to Scanderun, between Ashat and Elmanas.

AIN ES SALAH, or ENSALA, a district and the easternmost town in the oasis of Tewat in the Sahara. It is in N lat. 27 deg. 11 min, E long. 2 deg. 15 min. The inhabitants are a mixture of Arabs, Tuaricks, and Negroes.
AIN-EL-SHAGH, a fountain in the Great oasis, 20 m. N of El Khargeh, on the route to Siout on the Nile.

AIN SHEMS, a spring in the neighbourhood of the v. of Matariyeh, 2 hours S of Cairo. Though reputed the only real spring in the valley of the Nile, it is supplied like the other wells of Egypt by filtration from the river. [Wilkinson.] The city of Heliopolis-a name nearly identical with that of Ain- Shems, which signifies 'the Fountain of the Sun'-stood in the neighbourhood of this spring. Abdullatif, writing at the close of the 12th cent., describes A. as "a small town which was surrounded by a wall still easily recognised though in ruins." -Also a v. of Syria, 15 m. W by S of Jerusalem, supposed to mark the site of the ancient Bethshemesh.
AIN EL TAKA, a large spring in that part of the vale of the Orontes called the Ghab. It forms a lake, called El Taka, about 4 m. in length, and of irregular breadth.

AIN TAKKAZIE, a small lake of Abyssinia, in about N lat. 11 deg. 42 min., E long. 39 deg. 20 min., on the N side of the Lasta mountains, which receives the water of three springs, and gives birth to the Takkazie, the second river in Abyssinia.

AIN TERAZ, a convent and college of the Greek Catholic church, in the Lebanon district of Syria, on a height overhanging the banks of the Nahr el Kádhí, 3 m, N of Jisr. This establishment suffered greatly during the Druse war.
AIN YAMEN, a spring on the W side of Wadi Arabah, at the N end of a deep defile which leads through the frontier mountains of Judah into a wide sandy plain.-Bertou.
AIN YELA, a halting-place in Tafilet, in the district of Gourland, 20 m. S by W of Afileh, on the route pursued by M. Caillié on his return through Marocco.
AIN ZELAND, a halting-place on the same route, 85 m. N of Ain Yela.

AIN AD, or AINAD, a town of Arabia, in the prov. of Hadramaut, 13 days' journey from Keshin and 7 from Shehr. Niebuhr says an inhabitant of this town told him that the tomb of the prophet Kachtan or Jaktan stands within a day's journey of A.; that many pilgrims visit it; and that a famous fair is held at it. Wellsted was informed that A. is in size nearly equal to Terim, which is the largest town in Yemen, and that it probably contains 10,000 inhabitants.

AINADA, or Iniada, a port of Asiatic Turkey, in the Romelian sanjak of Kirkkilisa, at the head of a bay on the coast of the Black sea, formed between capes Ainada and Mediyah, 75 m. NW of Constantinople.—Cape A. is in N lat. 41° 53', E long. 28° 3', bearing NW 1/2 N from the entrance of the Bosphorus. It is sometimes called CAPE KOURI.

AINADJIK, or AINEDJIK, a town of Turkey, in the prov. of Romelia, on the road from Constantinople to Selánik. AINAGOUL, AINE-GUEUL, or AINEH-GOL, a town of Asiatic Turkey in Natolia, in the sanjak of Aidin, 12 m. SE of Philadelphia, on the r. bank of a stream supposed to be the ancient Cogamus.
AINAY-LE-CHATEAU, a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Allier, and arrond. of Montluçon, on the Sologne. Pop. 1,147.
AINCILLE, a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Basses Pyrénées, arrond. of Mauléon, 12 m. SE cf St. Jean-Pied-de-Port. Pop. 558. There are salt springs here.
AINCOURT, a v. of France, in the dep. of Seineet-Oise, arrond. of Mantes, 5 m. S of Magny.
AINDERBY-STEEPLE, a parish in the N. R. of York. Area 4,300 acres. Pop. in 1841, 760.—The township of A. is 23 m. WSW of Northallerton.
AINEK, the capital of Beru in the Galla country N of Shoa. It is on the river Chaffa, which, flowing from the W, joins the Hawash in Adel.—Krapf.

AINETTAH, or AINNETE, a v. of Syria, in "a lateral gash" of the Lebanon, forming the boundary between the Lower and Upper Lebanon. [Wilson.] Russeger estimates its alt. at 4,989 Paris ft., or 5,316 English ft. above sea-level. Dwarf oaks cover the mountain up to this point; but Burckhardt required 25 hours to reach the summit above the v., and a still higher summit lies half-an-hour to the right. Lake Limun lies 2 hours to the W.
AINHOUE, a com. of France, in the dep. of Basses | Pyrénées, arrond. of Bayonne. Pop. 853.

AINOS, or AINO, the name given to the aboriginal inhabitants of the islands of Saghalien and Jesso, N of Japan; and derived from a word in their own language signifying 'man.' The Japanese and Chinese regard them as perfect savages, and describe them as destitute of religious ideas, and covered with a coat of hair forming nearly as thick a fur as that of wild animals. Captain Broughton, who visited their islands towards the end of last century, says that "their beards were thick and large, covering the greatest part of the face, and inclining to curl;" and that their bodies, even in some children, were almost universally covered with long black hair. Captain Krusenstern speaks more favourably of them, describing them as of a middling height, nearly black, with bushy beards,— black, rough, lank hair,—and more regular features than the Kamtschatkans. Langsdorff also says of those Ainos whom he saw in Jesso; "the expression of their countenances was friendly and benevolent. They had tolerably large eyes, rather high cheek-bones, a somewhat broad and compressed nose, and among most the cheeks and chin were overgrown with long thick black beards." The Kurilians of Kamtschatka, Klaproth says, call themselves Ainu; those of the Kurile islands, Ainuh; those at Jesso and Tschoka, Aino; and those near the Amur.
AINSA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Huesca, on the Cinca, 22 m. N of Barbastro. It was in ancient times the residence of the kings of Aragon.
AINSER, a v. of Kurdistan, 5 m. N of Tiláberi, between that place and Khanek.
AINSLIE (LAKE), a sheet of water in the western district of the island of Cape Breton, which discharges itself into the gulf of St. Lawrence, by the Salmon or Marguerite river.
AINSTABLE, a p. and v. in Cumberland, 12 m. N by E of Penrith. Area 4,119 acres. Pop. 501.
AINSTY. See YORKSHIRE.
AINSWORTH, a manufacturing v. in the p. of Middleton, Lancashire, 8 m. N of Manchester. Pop. of the chapelry, extending to 1,090 acres, 1,598.

AINSWORTH, a harbour in Admiralty sound, in the strait of Magalhaens, in S lat. 54° 23', W long. 69° 34'. The mountains at the back of this harbour are capped by an enormous glacier which descends into the sea.—King.

AINTA'B, or ANTAB, a large garrisoned town of Syria, in the pashalik of Aleppo, supposed by some to be the ancient Antiochia ad Taurum in Comagena and the key of Syria on its northern frontier; 55 m. N of Aleppo, 65 m. E of Alexandretta, and 30 m. W of Bir on the Euphrates; in N lat. 36° 58', E long. 37° 30′. It is about 3 m. in circumference, and is situated on two hills and in the valley between them. A small river, the Sejur, flows near the town, and is conveyed to the higher parts by aqueducts carried round the hills which branch out from the river above the town. The inhabitants—who may be estimated at from 25,000 to 30,000 [Bowring]—for the most part live on the hills, and have their shops at the foot of the hill. These being built with flat roofs, a visitor may descend on them, and the covered streets by which they are divided, before he is aware. In fact, the houses extending in an amphitheatre of terraces around a hollow, the roofs of each range serve as streets to the range immediately above. On the N side of the town is a strong castle on an artificial elevation, with a deep ditch surrounding it, cut out of the rock; and within the fosse is a covered way, great part of which is excavated from the rock, and the rest built and arched with stone. On the S side is an extensive cemetery, which, at a distance, resembles large suburbs. A. is inhabited both by Alushta. AIO Mahommedans and Christians; the latter-as they are universally to the N of Aleppo-are Armenians. The chief trade of this town is in oil, grain, rice, coarse stamped calicoes, and coloured woollens; also bees' wax, scammony, leather, raw hides, and skins dyed red and yellow, forming what is called Turkey leather. Cotton is grown in the surrounding and dependent district, but it is of coarse quality; the goat's wool, however, is fine. The ferdeh or capitation tax of the district of A. was levied in 1836 upon 11,323 persons, and yielded 466,255 piastres. [Bowring's Report.] A. is governed by a mutzeim appointed by the Turkish government at Constantinople. Many medals of the Syrian kings and those of Cappadocia have been found in this town. The journey from Aleppo to A., and back again, is accomplished by camel-caravans in 6 days; and the rate of carriage is from 80 to 120 piastres per cantar of 187 okes; or 18 to 27 p. per cwt.—Bowring's Report on Syria.— Colonel Mackintosh.—Browne's Travels.—Maundrell's Travels.

AINUNAH, a harbour on the coast of Arabia, in N lat. 28° 2', E long. 35° 18', at the mouth of a wadi celebrated among the Bedouins for the abundance and purity of its water, and presenting, in its luxuriant vegetation, a singular contrast to the surrounding sterility. There are some ruins on both sides of the valley.—Wellsted. AINZERBEH, ANZARBA, or ANAZARBA, a ruined city of Asiatic Turkey, in the pashalik of Itchil, on the Jihun river, 42 m. SW of Marasch. In the reign of Nerva, this city was devastated by an earthquake; and again successively in the reigns of Justin and Justinian. In the 5th cent. A. was made the capital of the second division of Cilicia, and its bishop received the rank of metropolitan. In 1130 the Saracens defeated the Christian forces in the neighbourhood of A. Colonel Chesney says "the ruins of the city are still extant, backed by an isolated mountain bearing a castle of various architecture."
AINZON, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Saragossa, 2 m. SE of Borja.
AIO AIO, or Ayo Ayo, a town of Upper Peru, 36 m. S of La Paz, on the road to Sicasica. Lat. 18° S.
AION, or AIUN, an island of Siberia, lying in the mouth of Tchaun bay, having its N point in N lat. 69° 57', E long. 168° 40'. [Wrangell.] It is called SABADEI by the Chukchi.
AIOS. See Agios.
AIOS ELIAS, a mountain on the northern frontier of Greece, near the W extremity of the chain of Othrys, at the head of the basin of the Sperchius.

AIOU, a group of islands in the Papua archipelago, off the N coast of Waygiou. They are surrounded by a reef which has a deep channel on the NW side. Tropical fruits are abundantly produced on these islands; but the inhabitants are chiefly supported by fishing. Aiou Baba, the largest of the group, is about 5 m. in circumference, and rises in the centre to an alt. of 500 ft. It is in N lat. 0° 21', E long. 131° 0'.—Raper, 3d. ed.

AIOUDAG, or AJOUDAGH (CAPE), a steep promontory on the S coast of the Crimea, forming the W side of the bay of Lampad; in N lat. 44° 33', E long. 34° 20'. From the offing to the E, it has the appearance of an island. It is known to the seamen of the Mediterranean by the name La Camilla, or 'the Camel;' but its name signifies 'the Bear's hill.' Pallas describes it as a lofty conoidal hill, thinly covered with trees, and separated from the Yaila, or Alpine ridge behind it, by the valley of Yursuf. It commands a prospect of nearly the whole southern coast of the peninsula, and may be seen from near Sudagh as well as from Gaspra. It is 12 versts from
AIOURENDI, or IURENDI, a town of Asiatic Turkey, 28 m. E of Edremid, at the E foot of the Musluk Dagh, on the road to Balikesri. AIO VASSILI, Agios Vasilios (Cape), or Aiva-
SIL BURUN, a promontory on the south coast of the Black sea, in Romelia; in N lat. 40° 56', E long. 38° 17'.—Kiepert.
AIPE, a town of Cundinamarca, at the influx of a small river of the same name into the Magdalena, on the l. bank, 110 m. SW of Bogota.

AIR, or AYR (POINT OF), the NE extremity of the Isle of Man; in N lat. 54° 25', W long. 4° 22'; 22 m. SSE from the Mull of Galloway; 29½ m. W by N ¾ N from St. Bees in Cumberland; 4 m. E & S from Rhu point. There is a light on this point, revolving once in 2 m., showing white and red alternately, at 106 ft. above the sea.—There is a point of the same name, on the coast of Flint, at the W entrance of the Dee, in N lat. 53° 22', W long. 3° 19'. A light-tower with one fixed bright light at 42 ft. above high tide is placed on this point close to low water mark.
AIRAINES, a com. of France, in the dep. of La Somme, arrond. of Amiens, between Amiens and Abbeville. Pop. 1,967.
AIRASCA, a small town of Piedmont, 9 m. ENE of Pinerol.
AIRAVATI. See ERAWADI.

AIRD (THE), a small peninsula on the E coast of the island of Lewis in the Scottish Hebrides.—Also a district of Inverness-shire in the vale of the Beauly.—Also a district of Appin, in Argyllshire, between lochs Linnhe and Crinan.—Both the NE and the SE extremities of the isle of Skye also bear this name, which is indeed of frequent occurrence in Celtic topography, under the form aird, or, more commonly ard, signifying a point, promontory, or rising ground.
AIRDLE, a tributary of the Erroch river in the NE of Perthshire, flowing SE through the strath of the same name.

AIRDRIE, a town and parliamentary burgh in the p. of New Monkland, in Lanarkshire, 11 m. E by N from Glasgow, and 32 m. W by S from Edinburgh. About a century ago it was little more than a solitary farm-hamlet; in 1841, the pop. within the parliamentary boundaries was returned at 12,418. It owes its rapid growth chiefly to the working of the extensive and rich beds of ironstone and coal which surround it. It was erected into a free burgh-of-barony in 1821.
AIRD'S MOSS, an extensive tract of elevated marshy moorland in Ayrshire, lying between the water of Ayr and the Lugar, chiefly in the p. of Auchinleck. It is about 5 m. in length, by 2 in breadth.

AIRE, a river of Yorkshire, which rises in Malham tarn, 6 m. E by N of Settle; runs ESE past Skipton, to Leeds; receives the Calder from the W 12 m. below Leeds; turns E and then NE, and joins the Ouse 3 m. SW of Howden. It has been made navigable to Leeds for vessels of 100 tons burthen.

AIRE, a river of France, which rises near Ligny in the S of the dep. of the Meuse, passes Clermont and Varennes, receives the Chiers, and joins the Aisne on the r. bank, 9 m. below Vouziers, after a course of 60 m., a great part of which is parallel to the Meuse.

AIRE-SUR-L'ADOUR, a cant., com., and city of France, in the arrond and 14 m. SSE of St. Sever, in the dep. of Landes. The city stands on a rising ground on the l. bank of the Adour. Pop. of town and com. 4,433. It is the seat of a bishop, suffragan of Auch, whose diocese comprehends the dep. of Landes. A cathedral, college, and secondary ecclesiastical seminary exist here. A. was the Vicus Julii Atura and Atures of the middle ages; and was the residence of Alaric II., king of the Visigoths. The cant. comprises 14 com. Pop. 11,680 in 1831.

AIRE-SUR-LA-LYS, a cant., com., and town of France, in the arrond. and 10 m. SE of St. Omer, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais. The town, which is well- AIS built and fortified, stands in a low marshy district upon the Lys, which is joined by the canal d'Aire-ala-Bassée, passing Béthune and La Bassée, to the Deule. Pop. of town 9,000. The cant., comprehending 14 com., had a pop. of 16,905 in 1831. A. was taken by Marshal de la Meilleraye in 1641, and by Marshal Humières in 1676.

AIRIAKI, or Molotschnaia, [i.e. 'the River of Milk,'] a river of Russia, a branch of the Surgut, in the gov. of Simbirsk, which takes its rise in a lake whose waters, strongly impregnated with sulphur, give it a milky tinge in the higher part of its course.
AIRLIE, a p. in Forfarshire, stretching along the river Isla. Area 6,000 acres. Pop. 868.
AIROLA, a town of Naples, in the Terra di Lavoro, on an abrupt eminence 4 m. W of Monte Sarchio. Pop. 3,900.

AIROLO, or ERIELS, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Tessin, on the l. bank of the Tessin or Ticino, at 3,794 ft. above sea-level. [Pini.] Pop. 1,800. The pass of St. Gothard, or rather that part of it called 'the Last step of the Mountain,' commences at this place, and the summit may be reached from it in two hours. On the 13th of September, 1799, the Russian general Suvárov defeated the French at this v. and forced the pass of St. Gothard.
AIRTH, a p. in Stirlingshire, on the frith of Forth. Pop. 1,498. The town of A. is 5 m. N of Falkirk. Pop. 583.
AIRTHRIE, a hamlet in Stirlingshire, in the immediate vicinity of Bridge of Allan, celebrated for its mineral spring, a pint of which holds 73 grains of mineral salts in solution.
AIRVAULT, a cant. and town of France, in the dep. of Deux-Sèvres, arrond. of Parthenay, on the Thouay, 39 m. N by E of Niort. Pop. 1,957. It suffered during the civil wars of the 16th cent.

AIS, or BURNT ISLAND, a small rocky islet, in the gulf of Bab-el-Mandeb, off the Somauli coast, in N lat. 11° 14', E long. 47° 19'. Vessels making for Aden, or entering the Red sea, with the SW monsoon, generally stretch across to Aden from a little to the E of this island.
AISCH, a river of Bavaria, rising in the Aischloch near Oberscheckenbach, in the circle of Middle Franconia, and flowing N into the Regnitz, 12 m. S of Bamberg, after a course from SW to NE of 40 m.
AISCHUM, a county in the NW part of Michigan, U.S., comprising 576 sq. m., and watered by the Notipekago and branches of the Manistee.
AISEAU, a com. and v. of Belgium, in the prov of Hainault, 8 m. E of Charleroi. Pop. 292.
AISEY-SUR-SEINE, a v. of France, in the dep. of Côte-d'Or, arrond. of Chatillon, on the Seine.
AISLINGEN, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of the Upper Danube, 4 m. S of Dillingen. Pop. 920. The district of A. was in early ages a free county of the empire, in the bishopric of Augsburg.

AISNE, a river in the NE of France, which takes its rise near Beaulieu, in the dep. of the Meuse; runs in a NW direction through a small portion of the dep. of the Marne, into that of the Ardennes; then bends its course towards the W, and, after traversing the dep. to which it gives its name, enters that of the Oise, and falls into the river Oise a little above Compiegne. It passes, in its course of nearly 150 m., the towns of St. Menehould, Mouron, Vouziers, Rethel, Château - Porcien, Neufchatel, Pontavaire, Vailly, Soissons, and Vic. It receives, on the r., the Aire and the Vaux; and on the l. the Retourne, the Suippe, and the Vesle. It is navigable as far as Neufchatel, or for nearly one half of its course; and is connected with the Somme by means of the canal of Crozat, and also with the canal of Ardennes. The principal articles of which it forms a medium of transit are, wood for fuel, charcoal, grain, iron, slate, marble and wine.

AISNE, a dep. of France belonging to the basin of the Seine; composed of the entire districts of Vermandois and Thiérache, the larger portion of Lammais and Soissonnais; and the districts of Brie and Galvèse, which respectively constituted parts of the old provinces of Picardy, the Isle of France, and Champagne. It lies between the parallels of 48° 50' and 50° 4' N; and is bounded on the N by the dep. Du Nord; on the NE, at one point, by the Belgian prov. of Hainault; on the E by the dep. of the Ardennes and of the Marne; on the S by those of the Marne and of the Seine and Marne; and on the W by the dep. of the Oise and Somme. It presents a triangular outline, with the apex pointing southwards. Its length from N to S is 90 m.; its greatest breadth from E to W 60 m. Its superficial area is 728,530 hectares, or 1,780,300 acres. It has in general an undulating surface; and is traversed in its central and southern districts by ranges of hills running E and W, whose greatest height does not exceed 650 ft. above the level of the sea. It is watered in the same regions by the Marne, which forms the principal line of separation from the adjacent dep. of the Seine and Marne, and by the Ourcq. The river from which the dep. derives its name crosses it in an easterly direction. In its northern districts it is intersected by the Oise, Somme, Scheldt, and Sambre, which take their rise in its NE angle. The Aisne is connected with the Somme by the canal of Crozat; that of St. Quentin unites the Upper Somme and Scheldt; the canals of Manicamp and the Oise are prolongations of the canal of Crozat. Numerous small marshy swamps or lakes-of which that of St. Laurent after rains presents a superfices of 220 acres -occupy about 6,000 acres; and one-seventh of the entire surface is covered with wood, consisting chiefly of oak, yokeelm, beech, ash, and birch. The soil, which is calcareous or chalky, is generally well-cultivated and productive. The plateaux and valleys are extremely fertile. The climate is cold and damp. The prevailing winds are the N and S.—The dep. contains no metallic veins capable of being wrought to advantage; but it abounds in freestone, marbles, potter's-clay, alum, pyrites, gypsum, and peat, of which last a tract of 650 acres in extent occurs between Laon and Rheims. About five-sevenths of the entire surface are devoted to the culture of grain. Meadow-land occupies one-seventeenth, and about a seventy-seventh part is appropriated to the vine, the cultivation of which is confined to the districts S of Laon, and to the banks of the Aisne and Marne. The fattening of cattle, and the rearing of horses and poultry, form an important part of the rural industry of this dep., which is conducted on the enlightened principles of agricultural economy common to the northern districts of France. The number of sheep is estimated at 700,000; that of horses at 69,414; and that of cattle at 85,000. The forests afford shelter to numerous deer and beasts of the chase. Aquatic birds abound in the marshes; craw-fish of large size are abundant in the rivers. St. Quentin—formerly famous for its cotton and linen fabrics, table-cloths, &c.—continues the principal seat of the manufacturing industry of this dep. The spinning of yarn affords extensive employment in the northern districts. The glass-works of St. Gobain are the most celebrated in France, and with those of Folembray, and chemical works, bleaching fields, oil works, sugar-houses, iron works, brick and tile kilns, corn-mills, &c., constitute the principal industrial occupations of the inhabitants.—The dep. is divided into the 5 arrondissements of St. Quentin, Vervins, Laon, Soissons, and Château-Thierry; which, in 1836, were subdivided into 37 cantons, and 840 AIV communes.—The principal towns are Laon, Chauny, Château-Thierry, Soissons, Saint-Quentin, Bohain, Fresverry-le-Grand, Guise, St. Michel au Tieracke and Le Monoin. The dep. of A. belongs to the military division of Paris. Its tribunals are subject to the court of Amiens. It forms the diocese of Soissons; and its bishop is a suffragan of the archbishop of Rheims. It is connected with the academy of Amiens, and possesses 5 colleges—one in each arr., a diocesan seminary at Soissons, 3 secondary ecclesiastical schools-Laon, Lusse, and Oulchy, and a normal primary school at Laon. The dep. likewise forms part of the forest-arrondissement of Douai. It returns 7 deputies; and the number of electors, in 1834, was 2,541. The territorial revenue in 1842 was estimated at 26,800,000 francs; the government revenue in 1844 amounted to 18,825,191 francs, of which 7,673,723 were direct taxes. The pop. in 1836. was returned at 513,000; in 1846 at 557,422, being a pop. of 76.51 to the square kilometre. Of this pop. 20 in 100 live in towns and villages; the remaining four-fifths are rural. A. ranks as the fourth dep. of France in extent of pop., and the thirteenth in superficial area.—Journal des Mines, Tom. v.—Brayer, Statistique du dep. de l'Aisne, 2 vol. 4to., 1824-25.—Annuaire de l'Aisne.-Patria.
AISONVILLE, a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Aisne, arrond. of Vervins. Pop. 830. A considerable quantity of woollen goods is annually manufactured here.
AIST, a river of Austria, formed by the junction of the Feld-Aist, and the Wald-Aist, and flowing into the Danube on the l. to the E of the Enns.
AISTERHEIM, a town and castle of Austria, on the Enns, SW of Grieskirchen.
AISTHORPE, or East Thorpe, a p. in Lincolnshire, 61 m. NW of Lincoln. Area 1,150 acres. Pop. 82.
AITAU, a chain of mountains in Independent Tartary, under the parallel of 50° N.
AITE, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Agra, 18 m. SSW of Jalun.
AITER, or AITRACH, a town of Würtemberg, in the circle of the Danube, on the Aiterach or Aitrach, a branch of the Iller.

AITO, or AETO (MOUNT), [i. e. 'Eagle's cliff,'] a precipitous conical hill on the island of Ithaca, rising on the isthmus which connects the northern and southern portions of the island, to an alt. of 400 ft. The bay on either side derives its name from this hill. The summit bears a mass of Cyclopean ruins called 'the Castle of Ulysses,' and commands an extensive view over the Ionian sea to the shores of the Morea.
AITON (Mount), a hill in New S Wales, 225 m. W of Sydney, in S lat. 33° 53', E long. 146° 35'.—There is another hill, sometimes called Alton's Peak, in S lat. 32° 55', E long. 150° 4'.

AITUTAKI, one of the Hervey or Cook's group of islands in the S Pacific; in S lat. 18° 54', W long. 159° 32'. It is 9 m. long from NW to SE, and rises 360 ft., presents rich and varied scenery, and is conjectured to support a pop. of about 2,000.

AIVA'LI, or KIDONES, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in the sanjak of Karahsi, 10 m. NW of Aiasmun, on the probable site of the ancient Heraclia. When Mr. Jowett visited this town in 1818, it was a flourishing place, with an industrious pop., chiefly Greeks, estimated at 25,000: it had a handsome college, built in 1803, a library, printing-press, two noble hospitals, 40 oil-mills, 30 soap manufactories, several tanneries, and most of the means and appliances of a flourishing manufacturing and trading town. But on the 13th of June, 1821, the possession of A. was struggled for by the Greek and the Turkish forces, and the town was reduced to a mass of naked roofless walls When Arundell visited the place in 1830, the pop. | was returning, and was calculated to amount to nearly 10,000.
AIX, a name given to many different places in Europe where there are mineral springs,-a contraction perhaps of the Latin aquas.

AIX, a town of France, the capital of the arrond. of the same name, in the dep. of Bouches-du-Rhone. It is situated on a plain, at the foot of some small hills near the river Arc; in N lat. 43° 32', E long. 5° 25'; 345 m. SSE of Paris, and 15 m. N of Marseilles. It is a handsome well-built town, and is still adorned with many relics of its ancient origin. Its streets are straight, well-paved, and well-lighted. The Orbitelle, or principal promenade, is lined with elegant mansions, and decorated with trees and fountains; and the Place des Prêcheurs is planted with elms, and has a fine jet d'eau in the centre. The most remarkable buildings are the cathedral of St. Saviour, a magnificent structure of the 16th cent., which, with its beautiful baptistry reared from the ruins of a Roman temple, and curiously carved gate of walnut-wood, is still in good preservation; the mausoleum of the marquis d'Argens, erected by Frederick II.; and the town-hall, which contains a valuable collection of antiquities, and a library of 100,000 printed volumes and 1,100 manuscripts, founded by the marquis of Méjanes, and forming one of the richest literary repositories in France. In the market-place, opposite the townhall, is a fountain surmounted by a fine column; and near it is the clock-tower, a curious piece of mechanism, of the 16th cent. The palace in which the parliament of Provence formerly met, forms one side of the Place des Prêcheurs. The baths, which are in the centre of the town, are good and of modern construction. The university, founded by Pope Alexander II. in 1409, was destroyed during the Revolution; but the number of its literary institutions still entitles A. to the appellation of the Athens of the S of France, by which it was wont to be distinguished. It possesses a communal college, a diocesan seminary, and a secondary ecclesiastical school, schools of law and medicine, a school of design, a society of agriculture and of arts and sciences, and a chamber of commerce. A. is the seat of a court of justice, which comprises in its jurisdiction the dep. of the Var as well as that of Bouches-du-Rhône. It is also the seat of the archbishopric of Aix-Arles-et- Embrun, founded in the 18th cent., and which comprises, with the exception of the arrond. of Marseilles, the entire dep.—The commerce of A. consists chiefly in corn, olives, olive oil, dried fruits, wine, brandy, hides, wool, silk, and silk fabrics. The sweet oil of A. is celebrated. It has manufactures of woollen cloths, fine and common rateens, swanskin, calmuks, cotton, silk, velvet, leather, and hardware; and has four annual fairs. The pop. in 1789 was returned at 24,492; in 1841 it amounted to 26,698.—Aix is of great antiquity. Its origin is ascribed to Caius Sextius Calvinus, who founded a colony B. C. 120, and named it Aquæ Sextiæ, from the thermal springs which he discovered in this locality. Julius Cæsar formed a colony here of the soldiers of the 25th legion, and gave it the name of Colonia Julia Aquensis. Augustus named it Colonia Julia Augusta. It sustained successive ravages from the Burgundians, Visigoths, Saracens, and Normans; and ultimately shared the fate of the other towns of Provence. Regaining its importance, it became the residence of the counts of Provence. In 1481, it was reunited to the crown of France. It is remarkable for the number of eminent men to whom it has given birth; amongst whom may be mentioned Brueys, Tournefort, Vauvenargues, the two Vanloos, and Adanson.—The arrond. of A. has an area of 216,723 hectares; and comprises the cantons of Aix, Berre, Gardanne, Istres, Lambesc, Martigues, Peyrolles, Salon, and Trets, with a total pop. in 1836 of 104,510.—Porte, Aix Ancien et Moderne, 1833, 8vo.

AIX, an island of France, belonging to the dep. of the Charente-Inférieure, at the embouchure of the Charente, half-way between Oléron and the mainland, 14 m. NW from Rochfort; in N lat. 46° 03', W long. 1° 11'. On its S point is a fixed light, elevated 56 ft. Pop. 500, chiefly fishermen. It was taken by an English expedition under Admiral Knowles in 1757, and the fortifications destroyed, but it has been strongly fortified since.—Also a v. in the dep. of Drôme, 4 m. SE of Dril. Pop. 211.-Also a v. in the dep. of Nord, 12 m. NE of Douay. Pop. 987.

AIX, or AIX-LES-BAINS, a town of Savoy, in the bottom of a valley on the E side of Lake Bourget, 8 m. N by E of Chambery, celebrated for its sulphuretted hot springs, of the temperature of 112° to 117°, at an alt. of 823 ft. above sea-level. The two springs rise within 300 yards of each other, at the foot of a high calcareous mountain. The upper spring is called the Source de St. Paul, or Alum spring; the lower, le Bain de l'Eau de Souffre, or the Sulphur bath. The situation of A. is unfortunate, Bakewell remarks, as the place is extremely close and hot in summer, from its low position and the quantity of warm water constantly flowing through the streets. Pop. 2,882. A. was the Aquæ Gratianæ or Aquæ Allobrogum of the Romans, and retains many traces of Roman antiquity. It has often been nearly burnt down. The last accident of this kind happened in 1739.
AIX D'ANGILLON, a cant. and town of France, in the dep. of Cher, arrond. and 12 m. NE of Bourges. Pop. of town 1,380; of the cant., comprising 11 coms., 8,051 in 1831.

AIXE, a cant. and town of France, in the dep. of Haute-Vienne, arrond. of Limoges. The town is on the l. bank of the Vienne river, 7 m. SW of Limoges. Pop. 2,631. The cant., comprising 10 com., had a pop. of 11,352 in 1831.
AIX-EN-GOHELLE, a com. of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, arrond. of Béthune. Pop. 917.
AIX-EN-OTHE, a cant. and town of France, in the dep. of l'Aube. The town is 15 m. WSW of Troyes. Pop. 1,997. The cant., comprising 11 com., had a pop. of 8,326 in 1831.

AIX-LA-CHAPELLE (Germ. AACHEN), a fine old city, rich in historical associations, and of rising or regenerated commercial importance, the capital of the government of the same name, in the Prussian province of the Rhine, finely situated in a fertile "bowl surrounded by bold hills," between the Rhine and the Meuse, 81 m. by post-route E by S from Brussels; 168 m. by a continuous line of railroad from Ostend, and 44 m. from Cologne; N lat. 50° 47', E long. 6° 5'. Pop. in 1828, 36,829; in 1835, 38,383; in 1846, 48,557, of whom more than 46,000 were Catholics. It is divided into the inner and outer towns, by a wall with ten gates and nearly 2 m. in circumference. The outer ramparts are in ruins, but very tastefully laid out in walks and shrubberies. Its finest building is the cathedral, the nave of which occupies the spot on which Charlemagne erected a chapel-whence the adjunct to the original name of the city—as a burial-place for himself; the choir is a comparatively modern addition, begun in 1353 and finished in 1413. The sacristy possesses several reputed relics of Charlemagne, of which the most veritable perhaps is an immense ivory hunting-horn; it is rich also in relics of peculiar sanctity, which are exhibited only once in every seventh year, from the 11th to the 25th of July, and in 1832 drew together a concourse of 60,000 devotees. The town-house ranks next in antiquity to the cathedral, and contains some interesting specimens of old German art. Besides the cathedral, A. contains 18 churches and 3 monasteries, a Protestant church, and a synagogue. It is the seat of a bishop, of a court-of-appeal, and a tribunal of commerce; and has an exchange, a gymnasium, a school-of-arts, a picture-gallery, a theatre in a very handsome modern platz or square, and a public library of 10,000 volumes. The pop. is a mixed race, and a recent traveller declares that the inhabitants of A. "speak every language and none well."-The manufactures of A. are considerable and increasing. They consist of broad cloths, cotton, needle-work, needles, pins, jewellery, hats, soap, and Prussian blue. The foundation of the woollen manufactures in this city and its neighbourhood dates from the epoch of the revolution of the edict of Nantes, when the Protestants took refuge in Belgium, Germany, and England. The needles of A. have been celebrated over the continent since the introduction of the manufacture in the 16th century. About 2,000 hands still find employment in this art; but it formerly afforded occupation to three times that number. There are tanneries and print-works in the town; and coal is wrought in the neighbourhood. The monies, measures, and weights of Prussia are now in use, but custom still adheres in many transactions to the old franc of 100 centimes, and the reichstaller of 54 marcs = 2s. 9d. sterling. [Commercial Tariff.] -A. is celebrated for its mineral springs, which rise in the centre of the town, and present different degrees of temperature and strength. That known as the 'Source de l'Empereur,' or 'Kaisersbad,' has a temperature of 143° F., and a most nauseous taste, such, as one might expect from a mixture of gunpowder and rotten eggs, arising from its high impregnation with super-sulphuretted hydrogen gas. The baths are the property of the city, and are annually opened with much ceremony on the 1st of May.—Gambling is carried on to a great extent in A., this being the only town in the Prussian dominions where it is expressly sanctioned by government. The redoute of A. is perhaps the most sumptuous gambling-house on the continent. [Macgregor's Note Book.] - About half-a-mile from the gates of A., in the narrow valley of the Wurmbach, a tributary of the Roër, is the pleasant little town of Burtscheid or Borcette, with a pop. of 5,100, which may be regarded as a suburb of A. There are hot springs here also; one of which has a temperature of 158° F. The Cologne railroad has its western terminus here. It was executed to A. in 1841; and carried forward to the Belgian frontier in 1843. A., originally a Roman colony, but sacked by the Huns under Atilla, owed its restoration and future celebrity to Charlemagne, whose favourite place of residence it became, and who died here in 814. From 813 to 1558, it was the scene of the coronation of the German emperors; and the regalia were kept here till 1794, when they were carried to Vienna. The privileges which Charlemagne and his successors conferred on this city gave rise to the saying that "the very air of A. made free even the outlaws of the empire." Its citizens were exempt from feudal services, and from the tolls and taxes levied on travelling merchants. While the German constitution existed, A. occupied the second place on the Rhenish bench, among the imperial cities which had a seat in the diets of the circle of Westphalia. In this city were signed the treaties of May 2d, 1688, between France and Spain, and of October 18th, 1748, between the Powers engaged in the war of the Austrian succession. Here too was held a congress of importance, in October and November 1814, at which were negotiated those treaties between Austria, Russia, Prussia, France, and England, by which 'the Quintuple alliance,' as it is called, was introduced into the politics of Europe. Under Napoleon, A .- which had been ceded to France by the treaty of Luneville, February 9th, 1801-was the capital of the dep. of the Roer. A recent letter from this city says: "A discovery has just been made here of the highest interest in a religious and historical point of view, viz., the remains of Charlemagne. It is known that in the year 1000, Otho caused the vault of the emperor to be opened; and that Frederick I., on the 29th of December, 1165, took up the bones of this great prince after he had been placed among the number of the saints by Pope Pascal III. Frederick kept these mortal spoils in a chest. AJA The vestments and insignia of the emperor became the coronation-robes and insignia of the Franco-Roman empire; and in 1792, Francis II. invested himself with them as king and emperor elect; afterwards they were conveyed to Vienna where they are still preserved. But the relies of Charlemagne were lost, except one arm, which was enshrined in a reliquary, and they could never afterwards be found. A few days ago, however, the old chest was found in a place adjoining the sacristy, where it was left entirely abandoned in a dark closet." [Galignani's Messenger.]
AIX-LA-FAYETTE, a com. of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, arrond. of Ambert. Pop. 904.
AIZENAY, a com. and town of France, in the dep. of La Vendée, arrond. and 8 m. NW of Bourbon- Vendée. Pop. 3,519.

AIZIER, a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Eure, arrond. of Pont-Audemer, on the l. bank of the Seine, 21 m. from its mouth. A good deal of wine and cider is shipped here; and, in 1837, the tonnage touching at and leaving the quay amounted to 16,630 tons. AJA. Some names occasionally written with this form of orthography will be found commencing with AIA, AYA, or HAGIA, in the present work.
AJAB SHEHR, a v. of Persia, in the prov. of Azerdbijan, on the E border of the lake of Urumiyah, half-a-mile from Shíshewán.

AJACCIO, the capital of the island of Corsica, on the N side of the gulf of the same name; in N lat. 41° 55', E long. 8° 44' [Tranchot, 1837]; 155 m. SE of Toulon. It is finely situated on the W side of a spacious bay of the same name, between Cape Muro on the S, and Cape Sanguinara on the N, sheltered in all directions, except the SW, by ranges of lofty hills, and which forms one of the safest and most commodious ports in Corsica. The principal streets, which are well-built, intersect one another at right angles. The house in which Napoleon Bonaparte was born, on the 15th of August, 1769, forms one side of a court in the Rue Charles. The chief buildings are the cathedral, the town-house—a handsome but unfinished edifice --- and the citadel. A. has a commercial college, a nautical school, a library of 14,000 vols., a botanic garden, and an agricultural society. Its principal manufactures are leather and bricks; its exports consist of oil, wine, wax, oranges, and coral. There is a road from A. across the island, by Corté, to Bastia. A. is the seat of the bishop of Corsica, who is a suffragan of Aix and Vienne. The climate is extremely mild; but from the beginning of July to the end of September is reckoned insalubrious, and there is a deficiency of good water. The Campo di Loro, a plain in the immediate vicinity, is noted for its fertility. The pop. of A. was estimated in 1821 at 7,401; in 1832 at 8,920; and in 1841 at 11,266.—The origin of this town is ascribed to the Lesbians. It is identified by some antiquaries with the Roman Urcinium; but that town, it is more generally supposed, stood 9 m. to the N of Ajaccio. The present town was built by the Genoese in 1495.—The arrond. of A., which extends into the centre of the island, has an area of 205,402 hectares. Its pop. in 1832 was 43,882; in 1836, 46,383. It comprises the cantons of Ajaccio, Bastelica, Bocognano, Evisa, Sainte-Marie-et-Sicche, La Piana, Salice, Sari, Sarrola-et-Carcopino, Soccia, Vico, and Zicavo. These are subdivided into 111 parishes or communes. The lower districts of the Campo di Loro are extremely fertile; but generally A. and Sartene, formerly composing the dep. De Liamone, compose the less-cultivated part of Corsica. The contribution foncière, or land-tax, of the district, amounted in 1834, to 34,424 francs, or £1,365; while that of the whole island was £6,745.
AJAIN, a com. of France, in the dep. of La Creuse, arrond. of Guéret. Pop. 1,815.

AJAN, an extensive but nearly unknown district on the E coast of Africa, stretching from Cape Guardafui on the N, to the river Magadoxo on the S; and extending westwards to the Galla territories S of Abyssinia. With the district of Adel on the N, it is comprehended under the title of the Barra Somauli, or Land of the Somaulis. Its surface is generally low, sandy, and barren; but towards the N, which is hilly, there are some fertile valleys. Ajan possesses no known rivers of importance. Its commerce, which is chiefly confined to the towns of Berbera, Zeila, Harrar, consists in ivory, ambergris, ghee, coffee, gold-dust imported from the interior, and aromatics. It possesses an excellent breed of horses. It is inhabited by independent tribes of Somaulis, who profess Mahommedanism. AJANTA, AJAYANTA, or ADJUNTA, a town of Hindostan, 53 m. N by E of Aurungabad, at the head of a pass of the same name leading through the Berar range of mountains, in N lat. 20° 25', E long. 76° 12'. -About 6 m. from A. are celebrated cavern-temples of which Lieut. Alexander has furnished an account in the 2d vol. of the Asiatic Society's Transactions.
AJARI, a dependency of Maraghah in the Persian prov. of Azerdbijan, stretching along the Jaghatu, and containing a number of villages, of which Káshawer is the most important.—Rawlinson.
AJAT, or AJAC, a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Dordogne, arrond. of Périgueux. Pop. 902.

AJELLO, a town of Naples, in Calabria Citra, 15 m. SSW of Cosenza, with a strong castle. Pop. 2,600. -Also a town in Principato Ultra, 2 m. SE of Avellino.—Also a town and district in the Contéa principesca of Gorizia in Illyria.
AJI (CAPE), the S extremity of the island of Cephalonia, forming the W horn of the entrance to the harbour of Argostoli, in N lat. 38° 9′, E long. 20° 23½′.

AJISTA'N, a large straggling town of Irakajemi, 20 m. SSE of Mokhar. It is surrounded by gardens, and celebrated for its pomegranates.—Serjeant Gibbons in Geog. Journ., vol. xi., p. 137. AJMEER, AJMIR, or AGIMERE, a city and district of Hindostan, in the centre of the Rajputana territories; formerly subject to the Mahratta chief Dowlat Row Sindia; but ceded by him to the British in 1818. —The district is bounded by the principality of Marwar or Judpur on the W and N; on the E by the rajah of Kishengur's territory; and on the S by the unceded Ajmeer mahals or pergunnahs of Jypur. The great Arávulli mountain-range—which here breaks into diverging ranges—divides it on the W from Judpur; and its waters take a SE direction to the Bunas, a tributary of the Chumbul. The inhabitants are Rajputs, Jats, and Khairs. See Rajputana. The city of AJMEER-a word of doubtful derivation, pronounced Adjmhair by the natives—the capital of a large subah or province of the Mogul empire under Akbar, is situated in N lat. 26° 19', E long. 74° 40' [Tod], N lat. 26° 27', E long. 74° 46\frac{1}{2}' [Bengal Hurkaru, May 28, 1819]; 80 m. WSW from Jypur; 230 m. from Delhi; and 1,030 from Calcutta. It stands on the slope of a hill, or rather of three hills, which have an elevation above the city of from 600 to 700 ft., and are crested by a chain of fortifications. "The rock is in most parts quite inaccessible; it has an abundant supply of good water in all seasons, from tanks and cisterns cut in the live rock; there are bomb-proofs to a vast extent, and store-houses like wells, where corn, ghee, &c., used to be kept; and with very little improvement from European skill, it might easily be made a second Gibraltar." [Heber.] The valley, in the midst of which the city rises, is thickly wooded in all directions with neem, peepul, and tamarind trees, and contains several large sheets of water. A break in the surrounding chain of hills affords a view from the city of the open country to the southwards. When first taken possession AKA of by the British, in 1818, it was in a state of ruin and desolation in consequence of the oppressive rule of the Mahrattas, and their constant warfare with the Khairs, an intractable tribe inhabiting the surrounding hills and jungles; but since the British occupation of the district, the inhabitants have returned in great numbers, and "a beautiful and flourishing city has emerged from the ruins of the old one, with a pop. amounting, in 1830, to 25,000." "Streets, squares, and bazars," says Burnes, who visited it in 1830, "rise in it daily, and a general uniformity of plan has been maintained. Besides its numerous temples, there is more satisfactory proof of improvement in a uniform bazar which has been lately finished by its Brahmins at the suggestion of the government, where a great and annual fair has been established."-On the western declivity of the fortress stands one of the finest and most ancient specimens of Hindu architecture now extant. Colonel Tod considers it to have been a Jain temple. Its columns, he says, are "unique in design, and, with the exception of the cave-temples, probably among the oldest now existing in India." The remnants of the palace of Shah Jehan, a costly and beautiful edifice, still exist on the banks of the Burhá Pushkar, a lake about 18 m. in circumference, 6 m. to the N of the city. To the W of the city is another pushkar or lake, three-quarters of a mile in circuit, the sides of which are covered with ruined pagodas and sepulchres. But the tomb of Khwajah Mo'ayyenud-din, to which Akbar is said to have made a pilgrimage barefoot from Agra 230 m. distant, on the S side of the city, is the grand attraction of Mahommedan pilgrims from all parts of India to A.—The British cantonments are at Nussirabad, 14 m. SSE of the city. The road to them lies "over a sandy and rocky plain, bordered on each side by mountains which would have been picturesque had they had a less bleak and barren foreground." [Heber.] Major Rennell supposes that A. may mark the site of the Gagasmira of Ptolemy.—Asiatic Journal.— Heber's Journal.—Tod's Annals of Rajasthan.—Lieut. Burnes in Geog. Journ., vol. iv.
AJMERGUR, a town of Hindostan, in the Ruttunpur district of Gundwana, near the source of the Nerbudda, 40 m. NW of Ruttunpur, 190 S of Allahabad.
AJO (CAPE), a promontory on the N coast of Spain, in N lat. 43° 31½, to the eastward of Santander, and 3 m. from Cape Quejo.
AJOFRIN, a town of Spain, in the prov. of New Castile, and 12 m. SSE of Toledo. Pop. 3,350.
AJOMANIA. See Agia Mama.
AJOTLA, or AYOTLA, a town 7 leagues E of Mexico, on the road to Vera Cruz.

AJUSCO, a porphyritic mountain, 10 leagues S of the city of Mexico, rising 4,582 ft. above the tableland, which is elevated 7,470 ft. It is a volcanic mountain; and Latrobe says it is exactly upon the same line with Popocatepetl and Toluca. Its sides are clothed with dark pine-forests, and furrowed by deep barrancas. From its summit, the ocean in the vicinity of Acapulco can be distinguished.

AKABAH (GULF OF), or BAHR-EL-AKABAH, the NE arm of the Red sea, extending above 90 m. in a direction from S by W to N by E, and forming the SE boundary of the mountainous peninsula of Mount Sinai. Its entrance is nearly shut up by the islands of Tiran and Sinafar, under the parallel of 27° 55' N; the western side of the former island being only 4 m. distant from the coast of the peninsula, and its eastern only 2 m. from the W end of Sinafar; while extensive reefs shooting out from it on both sides leave only a channel of about a mile in breadth on the W side, and another about a quarter of a mile broad on the NE side. The latter is the safer channel, and leads up to Ras Furtuk, in N. lat. 28° 6', where the gulf is contracted to a breadth of 7 m., but above which it widens considerably. From the entrance of the straits to beyond Ras Furtuk, the coast is "low, sandy, and sterile, even beyond the usual desert-features of Arabia." [Wellsted.] At 33 m. distance from Tiran island, on the W or peninsular side of the gulf, and nearly 29 m. E of Mount Sinai, lies Dhahab, or Mersa Dhahab, [i. e. 'the Golden port,'] which some have erroneously identified with the Eziongeber of Scripture. To the N of this, the coast rises with a gradual slope to the distance of 2 m., when it meets the lower undulations of a range of irregular broken hills from 800 to 2,000 ft. high; while on the E side, a loftier ridge of mountains, the Jebel Antar, after coming down close to the coast, recede inland, where they rise into peaks, leaving elevated and gradually rising stony plains between them and the sea. The summits of these mountains often present remarkable colouring. Some are of a deep blue tinge, and others streaked with a brilliant red and violet colour. But the only traces of volcanic rocks which Burckhardt saw during his explorations of the peninsula of Sinai were near the harbour of Sherm, between Ras Mahommed and the coast opposite the island of Tiran. About 71 m. NNE of Dhahab is Ras Arsar, a low sandy point; 7 m. beyond which, in N lat. 28° 50', is another low sandy point called Warsut, off which vessels find good anchorage. Beyond Warsut the breadth of the gulf decreases from about 13 m. to 9 m., but it preserves its depth in mid channel of from 120 to 150 fathoms, beyond White cape, in N. lat. 29° 11'. Between White cape and Juzerat Farun, or 'Pharaoh's island,' in N lat. 29° 24', and 8 m. W 25° 40' S from the fort and v. of Akaba, the depth in mid channel is from 70 to 90 fathoms. Nearly opposite White cape, on the Arabian side, is a small island called Omasír. The island of Farun is a barren rock, about \( \frac{1}{4} \) m. long, and 300 yards broad. The Arab v. of Akabah, in an oasis or extensive date grove, is in N lat. 29° 28½, E long. 35° 6′ [Moresby], N lat. 29° 30′ 58", E long. 35° 0′ 30" [Ruppell]. There is a small square fort at this v., a few 100 paces from the sea, garrisoned by soldiers from Egypt, who serve to keep the neighbouring Arabs in awe, and to protect the annual Egyptian hadji. Over the adjacent country are scattered numerous mounds and ruins. An hour's distance to the S are the ruins of a smaller castle. From this v. the head of the gulf forms a circular bay, running 3 m. to the N and NW, and the same distance across. The coast at the head of the gulf is low and sandy, being the lower extremity of the immense sandy valley called El Ghor or Wadi Arabah, extending probably 120 m. to the southern extremity of the Dead sea, and varying from 4 to 8 m. in breadth, bounded on each side by high, dark, and barren mountains. A few m. from the S end of this wadi, a ridge of sand-hills called El Sateh, [i.e. 'the Roof,'] divides the waters which run N towards the Dead sea, from those which flow towards the gulf of A. M. Bertou appears to have descended from the top of this ridge, or from the point at which he first gained sight of the gulf, to the v. of Akabah, in 1 hour and 40 minutes. Laborde informs us that all along the shore at the northern extremity of the gulf of A., where the Wadi Arabah meets it, on digging holes a foot deep in the sand, at the time of ebb, they are instantly filled with the purest water, and that in this way the fortress of A. is supplied; Wellsted also says that water is everywhere plentiful and good; but Burckhardt says that the castle "has tolerably good water in deep wells." From Ailah or Elath, which stood at the head of the gulf, a great military road—of which traces still re- AKA main-led northwards to Petra, where it divided, and led, on one side of the Dead sea, to Jerusalem and Gaza, and on the other, to Damascus. From A. to El Arish the road lies N 31° W, and the direct distance is 116 m.; from A. to Gaza, it lies N 9° W, and the direct distance is 125 m. [Wellsted.] It has been suggested that the Indian mail might be transmitted by this route; but, besides being more difficult to protect, there is no safe sea-port in this direction on the Mediterranean, though anchorage may sometimes be obtained at El Arish. Few Arabs live on the W coast of the gulf N of Magnah, which is nearly opposite to Dhahab, owing to the scanty pasturage, during the summer-months only it is visited by fishermen and charcoal-burners. The eastern shores are possessed by the Toweirah Arabs as far N as the Wadi Mokabelat to the N of Ras Om Haye, the western extremity of the El Tyh ridge. N of this point, and 5 or 6 hours from the head of the gulf, begins the territory of the Heiwat Arabs, a fierce race Burckhardt], who likewise appear to be in possession of the W coast from Magnah to the hadji station of A. The castle of A. is situated at 12 hour's distance from the Western chain, down which the hadji route leads. This descent is very steep, a circumstance which Burckhardt supposes has probably given to the place, and the gulf, the name of Akabah, which, in Arabic, means 'a cliff' or 'steep acclivity.' It was the Sinus Ælaniticus of the ancients. Up to the time of Burckhardt, the geography of the gulf of A. was utterly unknown. On many of the best maps, even in the earlier part of the 18th cent., no such gulf is to be found. D'Anville, following a Turkish map, gave to its northern extremity a bifurcation, at one point of which he placed Elath, and at the other Eziongeber; and so late as 1826, this error was being perpetuated in the maps of some of our best geographers. The navigation of the gulf is dangerous from the violence of the northerly winds, which prevail during the greater part of the year, and which sweeping down the straight continuous valley that extends from the Dead sea to the entrance of the gulf, acquire extraordinary force and strength. The winds are also very inconstant, shifting suddenly from contrary points of the compass, as they sweep down the wadis on either shore. At times they feel sultry, and again a cooler gust will immediately succeed, in which the thermometer will sink several degrees. [Wellsted.] There is a great variety of fish in the gulf, and shellfish also abound. M. Bertou remarks that all the fish and shell-fish which he saw at Akabah were of a reddish colour; and that the strand, composed of minute particles of granite and porphyry, had the same reddish hue. Commander Moresby executed a stasimetric survey of this part of the Red sea in the years 1830-33, under the auspices of the East India company, which has been engraved on a large scale.—Moresby's and Ellon's Sailing Directions for the Red Sea, 1841.—Burckhardt.—Laborde, Voyage de l'Arabie Petra. Paris, 1830-34.—Stephens' Travels in the East. - Wellsted's Travels in Arabia.

AKABAH, a dangerous ascent in the Beni Abban mountains in Algiers, 6 m. SSE of the Beban or Gates, on the route commonly followed from Algiers to Constantina. It lies upon the narrow ridge of a high mountain, with deep valleys and precipices on each side, and leads up to the extensive and fertile plain of Majanah. [Shaw.]—There is another Akabah to the E of Constantina, on the road by which the French army approached that city from Bona, in the campaign of 1837, between Jebel Ainunah on the l., and Jebel Kesádah on the r. The French engineers estimated the Ras el Akbah, or head of the pass, at 828 metres, or <math>905\frac{1}{2}</math> vards above sea-level. The country to the W of the pass towards Constantina, is undulating, without a single tree or shrub, and unpicturesque but well-cultivated; and the road runs at the bottom of a valley bounded by round sloping AKABAH AL SOUGHAIR, [i. e. 'the Lesser hills. Akabah,'] a ridge of mountains on the W confines of Egypt, in the ancient Cyrenaica, running from S to N, rising 500 ft. above sea-level, and terminating in Ras-el-Kanaïs, the Hermaea Extrema of Ptolemy, in N. lat. 31° 15½', E long. 27° 52½'. It is the Catabathmus Minor of Ptolemy; and may be regarded as the first step of the vast stair, or succession of ascents, by which the traveller rises from the plain of the Egyptian delta to the great Cyrenean plateau.

AKABAH AL KEBIR, or AL SOLOUM, the second and greater descent-as the name implies-from the table-land of Cyrenaica to the level of the Egyptian delta eastwards. It is 140 m. to the W of the Akabah al Soughair, and is generally assumed by ancient geographers as the frontier of Egypt on the side of Tripoli. The canton of Jebel Akabah, throughout its whole extent, is, however, an independent territory occupied by the great tribe of the Oualid Ali Arabs. M. Pacho describes the ascent of the Akabah al Soloum, by a pass at its northern extremity, as "rising in undulations of progressive height; or sometimes presenting steep sides which the camel climbs with difficulty. The rock is generally of compact and testaceous limestone. Masses of freestone are found insulated upon the calcareous formation, or the latter is blended with the former. Shrubs covered the spots of earth, and filled the crevices of the rocks." The alt. of the chain at this extremity M. Pacho estimated at 900 ft. It rises immediately from the sea-shore, whence it runs in a SSE direction to join the heights which border on the Ammonian oasis, and to meet the range of the Lesser Akabah inclining to the SSW, so as to include a triangular plateau—the Jebel Akabah al Soughair— between them. On the summit, westwards, is a table-land extending a distance of 13 hours from SE to NW, to which the name of Zarah, or 'Field,' is given by the Arabs, and in traversing which, M. Pacho frequently passed large encampments of shepherds. The appellation El Soloum is taken from a large port of that name, 25 m. SSE of Cape Luko .- Voyage dans la Marmarique, la Cyrénaïque, &c. Par J. M. R. Pacho. Paris, 1827 29. AKABAH-EL-FEREIK, [i. e. 'the Rocky ascent over the Barrier,'] the ascent of a sandstone ridge in Nubia, running across the Nile from SW to NE, between 30 and 40 m. SW of Ibrim, opposite Ipsambul, near which, in the side of a perpendicular rock, there is the most perfect perhaps of all the ancient temples in Nubia. It is described by Burckhardt as of coarse execution, but "in as perfect a state of preservation as when first finished."-There are numerous Akabahs in the valley of the Upper Nile: as the Akabah-el-Benat in N lat. 20° 45′, the principal rock of which is micaceous schist and chlorite; the Akabah-el-Dulka above the fourth cataract; and the Akabah Guerri above the sixth cataract. lostiest of a group consisting of conical hills of quartz Black sea, 9 hours N by W of Uskub, in N lat. 41° reposing upon gneiss and mica schist, to the NE of Y arapasón, on the Kizil Irmák. It is the Hirka Dagh of Kiepert's map, and is to be distinguished from the Akajik Tagh, or Akadschik Dagh of Kiepert, the eastern extremity of a syenitic range, on the opposite side of the Kizil Irmak.—Ainsworth.
AKALIKATOBO, a rocky islet in the Corentyn Fiver in British Guayana, in N lat. 4° 40½', W long. 37° 39'.—Schomburgk.
AKAM. See AKIM. AKC

AKAOKTAONG, a woody promontory on the r. bank of the Irawadi, nearly opposite Senywa, 35 m. below the city of Prome. The channel of the river at this place narrows to <math>\frac{3}{4}</math> m. It is, however, deep and rapid. The name signifies 'the Hill of Customs' in the Burman language. - Crawford.
AKARA. See ACCRA.

AKARA, or Akora, a town of Affghanistan, on the r. bank of the Kabul river, 10 m. above its confluence with the Indus at Attock. A little below this town is the v. of Sydu, where 8,000 Sikhs under Bood Singh successfully defended themselves against 150,000 Mahommedans.

AKAROA, a harbour and settlement formed in 1840, at the mouth of a deep inlet of the same name, on the S shore of Banks's peninsula, in the middle island of New Zealand, in S lat. 43° 50', E long. 173° 10', about 150 m. N of Otago. There is a native v. here, and some Europeans connected with whaling establishments, also a few French colonists. Major Bunbury describes the harbour of A. as an estuary forming a basin which extends about 8 m. into the peninsula. It is surrounded by high mountains, precipitous at the entrance, but gradually sloping into a succession of hills clothed with verdure and timber to their summits.
AKARRI, a town of Greece, in Thessaly, 15 m. S by E of Trikkala, near the r. bank of the Fanari, and on the site of the ancient Achora.
AKASAKA, or Okasaki, a town of Japan, on the S coast of the island of Nifon, 14 m. ESE of Nagoya, at the head of an inlet from the E side of Mia bay.
AKASI, a town of Japan, in the island of Nifon, on the S coast, 68 m. WSW of Meaco or Kio, and opposite the island of Avasisima in the gulf of Osacca.

AKASSA, or Assaka, a river of Africa, rising on the edge of the Sahara, and flowing into the Atlantic 4 m. SW of Cape Nun. It is also called the WADI SHLEEMA.—There is another river bearing this name on the same coast, 31 m. further to the SW. This is probably the INUN of Borda, whose embouchure is marked by him in N lat. 28° 17', W long. 11° 32'. Both rivers have bars at their entrance, but deep water inside. The coast-line between them is a continuous sandstone cliff. It is doubtful on which of these rivers the town of Wad-Nun is situated.—Lieut. Arlett in Geog. Journ.
AKAVANSK, a v. on the S shore of Lake Van, facing the island of Ahktamar.
AKBABA, a v. of Asiatic Turkey, 1 hour from Begkos. It is situated in a romantic valley, and is celebrated for its cherries and chestnuts.
AKBAH. See AKABAH.

AK-BA'SHI LIMAN, a small bay on the eastern side of the Bosphorus, in N lat. 41° 24′, E long. 26° 57', on which the ancient port of Sestos is supposed to have stood. A v. called Tolova stands near the mouth of a small stream flowing into it.
AK-BIYIK BABA, a v. and kadilik in the Natolian sanjak of Sultán Oní, NW of Eski Shehr, on a stream flowing from the Ainehgol NE to the San-
AKCHAH SHEHR, or AKHISSAR, a small woodgarius river. built fishing-port of Anatolia, on the S coast of the ral slips here.
AKCHAH SHEHR, or SURE'H, a town and kadilik of Natolia, in the pashalik of Siglah. The town is on the S coast of the Black sea, at the mouth of a small stream, 27 m. SW of Eregli.
